challenges.title: The Golden Lady on Top
challenges.description: The Duomo's tallest spire is crowned by an iconic golden statue — but do you know her name?
challenges.question: What is the name of the golden statue that crowns the Duomo's tallest spire, added in 1774 and considered the symbol of Milan?

feedback.correct: Exactly! La Madonnina stands 108.5 m above Piazza Duomo. A Milanese tradition says no building in Milan may exceed her height — a rule respected until 1958 when the first skyscrapers rose.
feedback.incorrect: The statue is called La Madonnina (the little Madonna). Placed in 1774, she is gilded copper and stands 4.16 m tall. Milanese legend holds that she watches over the entire city.

challenges.arHint: Point your camera at the top of the Duomo's central facade and look for the glowing spire marker.

challenges.title: The Floor That Brings Luck
challenges.description: Hidden in the Galleria's mosaic floor is a tradition that tourists travel from all over the world to perform.
challenges.photoPrompt: Find the mosaic bull emblem on the Galleria floor and take a photo of it. Tip: look near the centre of the octagonal crossing.

feedback.correct: Well spotted! The bull is the emblem of Turin. Spinning on its heel three times is said to bring good luck — the mosaic is worn down from centuries of heel-spins.
feedback.incorrect: The mosaic bull represents Turin, one of the four Italian cities whose crests decorate the floor. The other three are Milan, Florence, and Rome.

challenges.arHint: Enter AR view and look for a highlighted circle at the centre of the octagonal crossing — the bull mosaic will be outlined.
challenges.location: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II — central octagon

challenges.title: From Royal Palace to Museum
challenges.description: Palazzo Reale once hosted kings and emperors. Today it holds a very different kind of treasure.
challenges.question: Palazzo Reale served as the seat of rulers for centuries. What major role does it play in Milan today?
options: Temporary exhibition museum and cultural venue

feedback.correct: Right! Since 1921 Palazzo Reale has hosted major art exhibitions. It is now one of Italy's most visited temporary exhibition spaces, with over 600,000 visitors a year.
feedback.incorrect: Palazzo Reale is now a cultural venue and temporary exhibition museum, hosting world-class art shows since its post-WWII restoration. It is not a government building.

challenges.arHint: Stand facing the Palazzo and activate AR — the facade will reveal layers showing its transformation across Medieval, Neoclassical, and present-day phases.
challenges.location: Palazzo Reale — main facade on Piazza del Duomo
